content.boundary: Portugal's non-structural achievement gains and the retention-reduction fiscal win are strongly evidenced, but TEIP's competency-gap-closing effect is only moderately evidenced, and the scenario's own mechanism field acknowledges its benefits are structurally bounded by the unaddressed selection age.
content.change_lever: Portugal's documented gains (approximately 31 PISA points / 0.3 SD between 2000-2018) resulted from curriculum standards clarification, increased school autonomy, and regular student assessment — not from changes to selection age — providing a template mechanism transferable in principle to Hungary's comprehensive-sector (non-tracked) majority.; TEIP-style targeted funding reduces student dropout and grade retention, but does not close competency gaps relative to non-TEIP schools, and no rigorous longitudinal evaluation of its long-term student-outcome effects exists.
content.name: Non-structural compensatory reform (leave selection age unchanged)
content.system_problem: Pursue equity and achievement gains through curriculum clarity, targeted (TEIP-style) funding for disadvantaged schools, retention reduction, and school-quality levelling, without altering the 10-12 selection age or track structure.
